Abstract
Recentadvancesinlargelanguagemodels(LLMs)havestimulated
growing interest in agent-based recommender systems, enabling
language-driveninteractionandreasoningformoreexpressivepref-
erence modeling. However, most existing agentic approaches re-
main predominantly user-centric, treating items as passive enti-
tiesandneglectingtheinterestsofothercriticalstakeholders.This
limitationexacerbatesexposureconcentrationandlong-tailunder-
representation,threateninglong-termsystemsustainability.Inthis
work,weidentifythisfundamentallimitationandproposethefirst
Tri-party LLM-agent Recommendation framework (TriRec) that
explicitlycoordinatesuserutility,itemexposure,andplatform-level
fairness.Theframeworkemploysatwo-stagearchitecture:Stage1
empowersitemagentswithpersonalizedself-promotiontoimprove
matching quality and alleviate cold-start barriers, while Stage 2
uses a platform agent for sequential multi-objective re-ranking,
balancing user relevance, item utility, and exposure fairness. Ex-
periments on multiple benchmarks show consistent gains in accu-
racy, fairness, and item-level utility. Moreover, we find that item
self-promotion can simultaneously enhance fairness and effective-
ness, challenging the conventional trade-off assumption between
relevanceandfairness.Ourcodeisavailableathttps://github.com/Marfekey/TriRec.

1 Introduction
Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable ca-
pabilitiesacrossawiderangeofdomains[ 16,24,25,42],fundamen-
tally reshaping how intelligent decision-making systems reason
[31,48] and interact with users [ 23,47]. In recommender systems,
these advances have stimulated growing interest in LLM-based
agentparadigms,whereagentsmodeluserbehaviorsthroughlanguage-
driven interaction and reasoning [ 4,38], enabling more expres-
sive and interpretable preference representations compared to tra-
ditional collaborative filtering approaches [ 2,27,37].arXiv:2603.10673v1  [cs.IR]  11 Mar 2026

Conference acronym ’XX, June 03–05, 2018, Woodstock, NY Gong et al.
However, despite these promising developments, most existing
agent-basedrecommendermethodslargelymirrorhuman-centered
decision-makingpatterns[ 14,44]andadoptapredominantlyuser-
centric modeling paradigm [ 17,26,28,34]. They primarily opti-
mize user utility while treating items as passive functional entities
[35,40], thereby failing to adequately account for the interests of
othercriticalstakeholders,suchascontentprovidersandplatform
operators [ 3,23,29,39]. Such a user-centric decision paradigm is
inherently unsustainable in multi-sided marketplaces. In particu-
lar,thepervasiveMattheweffectconcentratesexposureonalready
popular items [ 9], leaving long-tail creators with limited visibil-
ity [11,46]. This imbalance weakens creator incentives, leading
to creator churn and a subsequent decline in high-quality content
production [ 7]. Moreover, excessive optimization for short-term
user engagement exacerbates a winner-take-all dynamic, gradu-
ally homogenizing the content pool. Ultimately, this erosion of
supply-side diversity undermines user experience and harms the
platform’s long-term ecosystem health.
Although some recent methods introduce item agents or con-
tent agents [ 15,36,38,39], these agents typically function only
as information carriers or attribute descriptors. Their role remains
largely auxiliary, serving to better satisfy user preferences rather
than actively pursuing reasonable exposure or long-term benefits
on behalf of the items themselves.
To address this limitation at its root, we propose a Tri-party
LLM-agent Recommendation framework (TriRec) that explicitly
aligns the interests of users, items, and the platform, as illustrated
in Figure 1. The framework adopts a disentangled two-stage archi-
tecture that preserves user experience while empowering items
with agency to actively compete for exposure through personal-
ized self-promotion. At the platform level, global ranking is fur-
therregulatedbyincorporatingsystem-levelconstraints,ensuring
long-term fairness and ecosystem stability.
Stage1:Generativeitemself-promotion.Weempoweritemagents
with the capability to actively compete for visibility. Items are no
longerpassivecandidates;instead,theybecomeactiveparticipants
by generating personalized promotion tailored to the target user’s
interest preference. For example, the same CD player might em-
phasize “high audio fidelity” to musicians, “popular tracks” to stu-
dents, and “easy audio playback” to seniors. This mechanism not
only improves matching quality but also provides long-tail items
with opportunities to gain exposure.
Stage 2: Platform-led multi-objective re-ranking. After Stage 1
generatesahigh-qualitycandidatelist,theplatformagentperforms
sequential re-ranking to balance multiple objectives. Specifically,
for each candidate item, the agent considers immediate user rele-
vance, platform-level fairness and expected item utility. The plat-
form agent makes ranking decisions one position at a time, this
sequentialstrategyensuresthatrankingchoicesconsiderbothcur-
rent item exposure and long-term fairness.
Notably,incorporatingitemself-promotionledtoanincreasein
both the average exposure and click-through probability of items,
whilesimultaneouslyimprovingplatform-levelfairness.Atthesame
time,theintroductionofpersonalizedpersuasivecontentenhanced
recommendation accuracy on the user side. These results demon-
strate that our framework can simultaneously support the objec-
tives of users, items, and the platform.Our main contributions are as follows:
•We identify fundamental limitations of user-centric modeling in
recommendersystemsandintroducethefirsttri-partyLLM-agent
recommendationframeworkthatexplicitlycoordinatesusers,items,
and the platform through agent-based regulation of exposure
and fairness.
•We propose a two-stage pipeline consisting of (i) item-side per-
sonalized self-promotion to endow items with agency and alle-
viate cold-start effects, and (ii) platform-led multi-objective re-
ranking with exposure modulation to balance tri-party interests.
•Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate consis-
tent improvements in accuracy, group fairness, and item-level
utility,andfurthershowthat item self-promotion cansimulta-
neously enhance fairness and effectiveness, challenging the con-
ventional trade-off assumption.
2 Related Work
2.1 LLM-based Agentic Recommendation
The remarkable reasoning capabilities of LLMs have fundamen-
tally reshaped intelligent decision-making, stimulating a surge of
interest in LLM-based agent paradigms within recommender sys-
tems [12,23]. Unlike traditional filtering, these agents model com-
plexbehaviorsthroughlanguage-drivenreasoning,enablingmore
expressive preference representations [ 39,43].
Withinthisresearchfrontier,aprominentdirectionutilizesagents
ashigh-fidelitysurrogatestosimulatehumanbehavior[ 3,42].Frame-
works like Agent4Rec [ 37] and DyTA4Rec [ 28] incorporate socio-
logical traits and dynamic profile updates to capture user interest
evolution. To ensure long-term sustainability, the PUMA frame-
work [45] enables efficient migration of personalized prompt as-
setsacrossevolvingmodelarchitectures.Beyondsimulation,agents
functionasintelligentinterfacestoimprovedecisionqualitythrough
deliberatereasoning[ 14,26,34].MethodssuchasSTARec[ 29]and
ReasonRec[ 40]incorporatereasoningmechanismstomitigatehal-
lucinations and enhance robustness, while LatentR3 [ 41] further
optimizes efficiency by utilizing reinforced latent tokens instead
of explicit text generation.
Meanwhile,decentralizedmulti-agentarchitecturesdecompose
complextasksintospecializedrolesormodelbidirectionaldynam-
ics[17,27,36].FrameworkslikeAgentCF[ 39]andRec4Agentverse
[38]introduceitemagentstointeractautonomouslywithuseragents
forpreferencerefinement.Toaddressthelong-termimpactofthese
interactions, the bi-learning planner [ 20] coordinates macro-level
guidanceforsustainedengagement.However,existingmulti-agent
paradigms primarily focus on maximizing one-sided user utility
while neglecting the interests of the items. In this work, we pro-
pose a novel framework that explicitly models item agents and
equips them with the ability to advocate for their own visibility,
while maintaining alignment with user preferences.
2.2 Creator-side Recommendation
User-centric recommendation often causes most exposure to go to
afewpopularitems,leavingmanycreatorsandtheircontentinvisi-
ble. This lack of visibility hinder the creator experience, ultimately

ment[6,7].Earlymethodologiesprimarilyutilizedco-learningframe-
works to synchronize user and provider representations [ 6], or de-
veloped specific mechanisms like PRINCE [ 13] to provide counter-
factual interpretability for the provider side.
Recently,researchershaveexplored”mirroring”user-centrictech-
niques, with models such as DualRec [ 7] treating items as active
queries to identify suitable users and tackle the user availability
challengeindual-targetoptimization.Concurrently,generativeap-
proaches such as HLLM-Creator [ 5] have begun leveraging LLMs
to produce personalized creative content, thereby enhancing item
appeal through automated content augmentation. FlyThinker [ 22]
furtheradvancesthisbyenablingreasoningandgenerationtopro-
ceedconcurrently,allowingforthedynamicguidanceoflong-form
personalized responses. However, these frameworks remain view-
ingcreatorsaspassivetargetsforalgorithmicoptimizationorstatic
subjects of content generation and lack the explicit modeling of
autonomous agency. Different from these passive approaches, our
work empowers creators with agentic capabilities, enabling tai-
lored promotion of their content to users.
2.3 Platform Fair Ranking
The pervasive Matthew effect in recommendation leads to expo-
sure concentration, leaving vast long-tail products invisible and
causingcold-startproblems[ 21,32].Consequently,platformsmust
implement systematic interventions to reconcile the trade-off be-
tween accuracy and multi-stakeholder fairness [ 30]. Classical re-
search addresses this by formulating exposure allocation as a con-
strainedoptimizationproblem[ 19,21]orbylearningdebiasedrep-
resentations[ 18,30]toneutralizeprovider-sidebiases.Beyondstatic
debiasing,SPRec[ 9]introducesself-playmechanismstoiteratively
mitigatefilterbubblesandimprovefairnesswithoutadditionaldata.
Anotherdominantparadigmutilizespost-hocre-rankingtoachieve
equitable resource distribution via social welfare criteria. To ac-
count for temporal dynamics, models like LTP-MMF [ 33] intro-
duce mechanisms to mitigate the deleterious feedback loop effect.
Similarly, the bi-learning planner [ 20] utilizes LLMs to manage
macro-level guidance for optimizing long-term user engagement.
Recently, multi-agent social choice frameworks, such as SCRUF-D
[1],introduceagentstoarbitrateconflictingfairnesscriteria.While
these methods move toward decentralized decision-making, they
struggle to perceive fine-grained item-level signals. In this work,
we formulate platform regulation as an agentic re-ranking prob-
lem that enables adaptive coordination of relevance, fairness, and
item survival objectives.

addressedbyTriRecfromamulti-stakeholderandagenticperspec-

and the platform. We first introduce stakeholder-specific utility
functionstocharacterizetheirrespectiveoptimizationgoals.Based

exposure regulation are handled in a decoupled yet coordinated
manner.Finally,wepresenttheagent-basedpreferenceinteraction
mechanism that serves as the foundation of Stage 1 candidate con-
struction.

andtheplatform,whichtogethercharacterizetheoveralloptimiza-
tion target of the recommendation process.
•User Utility. Users primarily seek high relevance and engage-
ment quality [ 43]. At the platform stage, we define the user-side

Based on the above utility definitions, the overall recommen-
dation process in TriRec is realized through a two-stage agentic
pipeline. Specifically, Stage 1 focuses on constructing relevance-

semantic alignment via agent-based interaction and personalized

Stage 2 then operates on top of the Stage 1 outputs by treat-
ingrecommendationasasequentialcontrolproblem.Theplatform
agent observes the system state, including historical exposure and
relevance rankings, and performs state-aware re-ranking actions

to jointly optimize user utility, item exposure utility, and platform-
level fairness over time.
This decoupled design enables TriRec to separate semantic pref-
erence modeling from long-term exposure regulation, while main-
taining a closed-loop decision process that dynamically adapts to
evolving system states.
3.2 Agent-based Preference Interaction
Following AgentCF [ 39], we adopt an agent-based recommenda-
tion paradigm to model fine-grained user–item preference align-
ment.
AgentCF-style interaction training is used to construct and re-
fine preference representations for both users and items. Through
offline replay of historical user–item interaction episodes in train-
ing phase, user agents gradually accumulate preference memory,
while item agents build item-side semantic memory that captures
theirappealpatternsacrossdifferentusergroups.Theseinteraction-
induced preference states are stored in internal memory modules
and reused during inference for contextualized decision making.
Formally, let 𝒰denote the set of user agents and ℐdenote the
set of item agents. Each user agent 𝑢 ∈ 𝒰maintains a preference
memory ℳ𝑢, which is summarized into a user interest representa-
tionz𝑢. Each item agent 𝑖 ∈ ℐmaintains an item-side memory ℳ𝑖
together with structured item metadata representation x𝑖.
Given a user agent 𝑢and a candidate item set 𝒞𝑢= {𝑐 1, … , 𝑐 𝑛},
the goal of Stage 1 is to generate a relevance-oriented ranking list
by modeling semantic interactions between agents.
The user agent evaluates candidate items based on their gen-

rankingdecisions.Thisinteractionprocessisimplementedthrough

In our framework, we further extend this interaction layer by

4.2 Platform-Agent Multi-Objective
Re-Ranking
Although Stage 1 produces relevance-oriented rankings via agent
interaction, it does not explicitly regulate long-term exposure allo-
cation across items. We therefore introduce a platform agent 𝒜𝑝
that performs state-aware sequential re-ranking to balance user
utility, item utility, and platform-level fairness.
We model the platform module as a sequential decision process
operating on a dynamic system state.
Exposure as a Control State. In real-world recommendation
systems, the platform cannot directly control user feedback such
as clicks or purchases, but can explicitly regulate item visibility
through ranking positions and display frequency. Therefore, we
adopt exposure as the core system state to characterize the cu-
mulative visibility of each item across sequential recommendation
rounds.
Moreover, exposure exhibits strong temporal dependency and
accumulation effects, which can lead to long-term popularity bias
ifleftuncontrolled.Modelingexposureasadynamicstatevariable
allows the platform agent to explicitly track historical allocation
and perform state-aware re-ranking decisions that balance imme-
diate utility and long-term fairness.

Relevance–Fairness Gain Function. We compute relevance–

5.2 Overall Performance Comparison (RQ1)
ToanswerRQ1,weevaluatetheperformanceofourproposedTriRec

across four datasets. From the results, we observe that TriRec con-
sistently achieves strong performance across user-side relevance,
platform-level fairness, and item-side expected utility.
5.2.1 User-side accuracy. Intermsofrankingeffectiveness,TriRec
achieves the best or second-best NDCG and MRR scores across all
four datasets. Consistent improvements are observed on CDs &
Vinyl, Movies & TV, and Steam Games, indicating that the pro-
posed framework does not sacrifice relevance performance when

anism in Stage 1, which provides richer semantic matching sig-
nalsandenablestheuseragenttomakemoreinformedpreference

TriRec also demonstrates superior ranking accuracy, suggesting
thatallowingitemstoactivelycommunicatepersonalizedinforma-

5.2.2 Platform-level fairness. Regardingexposurefairness,TriRec
consistently outperforms most baselines across datasets. Notably,
on Goodreads YA and Steam Games, TriRec achieves lower distri-
butional and group-level unfairness, demonstrating its effective-
ness in alleviating exposure concentration.
Comparedwithcentralizedpost-processingre-rankingmethods
(e.g.,SCRUF-D)thatrelyonstaticfairnessheuristics,TriRecachieves
amorefavorablerelevance–fairnesstrade-off.Thisresultindicates
that incorporating fairness objectives into a sequential and state-
aware ranking policy enables finer-grained exposure regulation.
Furthermore,relativetomarket-levelbalancingapproaches,TriRec
reducesunfairnesswithoutnoticeablydegradingrankingaccuracy,
highlighting the advantage of integrating platform regulation into
the agent-based pipeline.
5.2.3 Item-side expected utility. TriRec consistently achieves the
highest item expected utility across all four datasets. These gains
demonstrate the effectiveness of combining item self-promotion
with exposure-aware re-ranking.
By allowing item agents to actively present personalized con-
tent and incorporating exposure saturation signals into ranking
decisions, TriRec improves long-term item-side utility without re-
lying on popularity-driven exposure amplification. This is partic-
ularly beneficial for long-tail items, which typically suffer from
limited visibility under user-centric recommendation paradigms.
5.2.4 Cross-domain consistency. Acrossallfourdatasetsspanning
e-commerce,entertainment,andsocialreadingdomains,TriRecex-
hibits stable performance improvements. Although absolute met-
ric values vary due to domain characteristics and interaction spar-
sity, the relative advantages of TriRec remain consistent. This sug-
gests that the proposed framework generalizes well across hetero-
geneous recommendation scenarios.

achievesamorebalancedoptimizationoutcomethanexistingagent-
basedandfairness-awarebaselines.Unlikepurelyuser-centricagent
models, TriRec explicitly models item-side utility and platform-

when the platform agent retains sufficient flexibility to activate

tain additional visibility. However, excessively large 𝛼maxreducesthe agent’s effective adjustment space, limiting its ability to pro-

operational flexibility and result in suboptimal coordination.
5.5 Cold-start Item Promotion (RQ4)
5.5.1 Experimental Setup and Case Selection. To examine the ef-
fectiveness of the proposed agent-based matching mechanism un-
der cold-start conditions, we conduct a qualitative case study on
a representative cold-start product from the CDs & Vinyl domain.
The selected target item (ID: B072F27GQ8) has zero historical ex-
posure in the training set and therefore lacks collaborative signals,
popularity priors, or interaction-derived embeddings. This setting
allows us to isolate the contribution of semantic interaction and
item-side self-promotion without interference from behavioral su-
pervision.
As shown in Table 4, the target item is initially inserted into
the candidate list at a random position (ranked 8th), reflecting the
typical initialization scenario for newly introduced items in large-
scale recommendation systems.
5.5.2 Item-side Self-Promotion Generation. During Stage 1 inter-
action, the item agent is activated to generate a personalized self-
presentationbasedonstructuredmetadataandtextualdescriptions
associated with the target item. Specifically, the agent highlights
salient semantic attributes such as country music style ,storytelling ,
andemotional depth ,whicharehighlyrelevanttothecurrentuser’s
preference profile.
Unlike traditional feature engineering or template-based con-
tentgeneration,thisself-promotionisproducedthroughlanguage-
drivenreasoning,allowingtheitemagenttoadaptivelyemphasize
attributesthataremorelikelytoresonatewiththeuser’sexpressed
interests. This process enables the item to proactively present its
value proposition in a personalized manner.
5.5.3 User-Agent Response and Ranking Adjustment. After receiv-
ing the item-side self-presentation, the user agent evaluates can-
didate items by jointly considering its preference profile and the
semantic content provided by item agents. As illustrated in Ta-
ble4, the target cold-start item receives substantially higher rel-
evance scores due to its strong alignment with the user’s interest
in narrative-driven country music.

match user intent.
Overall,thisanalysisverifiesthattheproposedStage1agentco-

6 Conclusion
In this work, we proposed TriRec, a tri-party LLM-agent recom-
mendationframeworkthatexplicitlycoordinatesusers,items,and
theplatform,addressingthelimitationsofuser-centricagenticrec-
ommendation paradigms. TriRec adopts a two-stage design: item
agents generate personalized self-promotion content to enhance
semantic matching, while a platform agent performs sequential
multi-objective re-ranking to jointly optimize relevance, item util-
ity, and exposure fairness.
Extensive experiments on four public benchmarks demonstrate
that TriRec consistently improves ranking accuracy, exposure fair-
ness, and item-side utility. Notably, item self-promotion simulta-
neously benefits both item exposure and user-side effectiveness,
challengingtheconventionaltrade-offbetweenrelevanceandfair-
ness.
Ourresultshighlighttheimportanceofexplicitlymodelingmulti-
stakeholder objectives for sustainable recommender systems and
show that platform-aware agent coordination provides a practi-
cal path beyond user-centric optimization. Future work will ex-
plorericherplatformobjectives,suchascreatorlifecyclemodeling.
We also aim to investigate more advanced agent-level interactionmechanisms, including negotiation, coalition formation, and long-
horizon strategic planning, to enable more adaptive and coopera-
tive multi-agent behavior.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a novel Tri-party LLM-agent Recommendation framework (TriRec) that addresses the limitations of user-centric agency in recommender systems. The framework proposes a two-stage architecture, where items are empowered with agency to actively compete for exposure through personalized self-promotion, and a platform agent regulates global ranking to ensure long-term fairness and ecosystem stability. The document highlights the importance of considering the interests of multiple stakeholders, including users, items, and the platform, to achieve a more sustainable and diverse content ecosystem. The proposed framework is evaluated through extensive experiments, demonstrating consistent improvements in accuracy, group fairness, and item-level utility. The document also reviews related work in LLM-based agentic recommendation and creator-side recommendation, emphasizing the need for a more comprehensive approach that balances the objectives of all parties involved.

structure.title: Related Work
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of existing agent-based recommender systems, which primarily focus on user-centric decision-making and neglect the interests of other stakeholders, such as content providers and platform operators. It proposes a novel Tri-party LLM-agent Recommendation framework (TriRec) that aligns the interests of users, items, and the platform. The framework consists of two stages: generative item self-promotion and platform-led multi-objective re-ranking. The document highlights the importance of considering the interests of all stakeholders in recommender systems and proposes a new approach that empowers items with agency to compete for exposure, while maintaining alignment with user preferences. The main contributions of the work include introducing a tri-party LLM-agent recommendation framework, proposing a two-stage pipeline for item self-promotion and platform-led re-ranking, and demonstrating consistent improvements in accuracy, group fairness, and item-level utility through extensive experiments.

nodes.title: Agent-based Preference Interaction
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a tri-party framework for agent-based recommendation, called TriRec, which aims to address the decline in content production and platform engagement. The framework empowers creators with agentic capabilities, enabling tailored promotion of their content to users. It also addresses the issue of platform fair ranking, where the goal is to reconcile the trade-off between accuracy and multi-stakeholder fairness. The document formulates the recommendation problem from a multi-stakeholder and agentic perspective, considering the objectives of users, items, and the platform. It introduces utility functions for each stakeholder and describes a two-stage agentic pipeline, where preference-oriented interaction modeling and platform-level exposure regulation are handled in a decoupled yet coordinated manner. The framework uses agent-based interaction to model fine-grained user-item preference alignment and enables item agents to actively generate user-conditioned self-promotion. The platform agent then performs exposure-aware re-ranking based on joint utility optimization, ensuring a fair and relevant recommendation process.

structure.title: Method
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a recommendation framework called TriRec, which aims to jointly optimize user utility, item exposure utility, and platform-level fairness over time. The framework consists of two stages: a user-item interaction stage that generates relevance-oriented rankings through agent-based preference alignment, and a platform control stage that performs exposure-aware re-ranking based on tri-party utility optimization. The user-item interaction stage uses large language models (LLMs) to enable item agents to generate personalized self-promotions, which are then evaluated by user agents to form ranking decisions. The platform control stage regulates exposure and fairness on top of the relevance-oriented rankings produced by the user-item interaction stage. The framework is designed to address the cold-start problem and provide high-quality semantic matching between users and items.

nodes.title: Generative Personalized Self-Promotion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a tri-party framework for agent-based recommendation, called TriRec, which aims to jointly optimize user utility, item exposure utility, and platform-level fairness over time. The framework consists of two sequential modules: a user-item interaction module for generative personalized self-promotion and a platform control module for exposure-aware re-ranking. The user-item interaction module uses agent-based preference interaction to model fine-grained user-item preference alignment, where user agents and item agents interact to generate personalized self-promotions. The platform control module performs state-aware sequential re-ranking to balance user utility, item utility, and platform-level fairness, using a position-aware marginal joint utility function to approximate the induced listwise objective. The document also discusses the use of large language models (LLMs) to drive the interaction behaviors of user and item agents, and the adoption of exposure as a core system state to characterize the cumulative visibility of each item across sequential recommendation rounds.

nodes.title: Platform-Agent Multi-Objective Re-Ranking
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a tri-party framework for agent-based recommendation, focusing on platform-agent multi-objective re-ranking. The main points covered include: introducing a platform agent to perform state-aware sequential re-ranking, modeling the platform module as a sequential decision process, and adopting exposure as the core system state to characterize cumulative visibility of each item. The document also covers state representation, action space, state transition, and platform optimization objective, which aims to maximize long-term multi-stakeholder utility by coordinating user relevance, item utility, and platform-level fairness. Additionally, it introduces a position-aware joint utility function and a relevance-fairness gain function to compute the utility of each item, and discusses user utility modeling through a multiplicative interaction mechanism.

nodes.title: State-Aware Joint Utility Scoring
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses a tri-party framework for agent-based recommendation systems, focusing on a platform-agent multi-objective re-ranking approach. The main points covered include: 

1. Introduction of a platform agent that performs state-aware sequential re-ranking to balance user utility, item utility, and platform-level fairness.
2. Modeling the platform module as a sequential decision process operating on a dynamic system state, with exposure as the core system state to characterize cumulative visibility of each item.
3. Definition of a state representation, action space, and state transition function to enable the platform agent to track historical allocation and perform state-aware re-ranking decisions.
4. Introduction of a position-aware joint utility scoring function to approximate the induced listwise objective, which combines immediate relevance and long-term fairness.
5. Discussion of user utility modeling, platform utility modeling, and exposure-aware item utility to incorporate long-term exposure regulation.
6. Description of a greedy sequential action generation approach to approximate the long-term control objective.
7. Formulation of the platform agent as a closed-loop decision system to perform adaptive, state-aware re-ranking.
8. Experimental setup, including dataset description, evaluation protocol, and research questions to investigate the performance of the proposed framework.
